index.title: ESRF.net — European Security & Resilience Community
index.description: ESRF.net is a decentralised community of 1,931 organisations across 30 European countries working to strengthen security and resilience through ten vital sectors and five moral principles.
index.hero_deck: Where governments set policy and international bodies coordinate, ESRF.net unites the businesses, institutions and citizens whose daily work quietly sustains our shared life — a decentralised framework powered by the organisations that drive the economy.
index.hero_caption: A community of practitioners, spanning thirty nations, bound by five moral principles.
index.foundation_sub: For local impact. For shared resilience. For a Europe that holds.
index.foundation_body_1: ESRF.net is a decentralised network built for, and by, the entrepreneurs with local impact. Funded by Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund, we believe that security is not just a mandate for governments — it is the shared work of practitioners, innovators and communities who strengthen Europe through cooperation every day.
index.foundation_pull: Security is not just a mandate. It is a responsibility we carry, together.
index.foundation_body_2: By building resilience from the ground up, we connect organisations in thirty countries into self-sustaining ecosystems with regional impact. The foundation channels donations, contributions and aligned capital towards initiatives that strengthen the network and expand its reach. When many contribute a little, the result is greater than any single investment.
index.foundation_body_3: Organisations are classified across ten vital sectors aligned with the EU NIS2 Directive and guided by five moral principles: vigilance, stewardship, empowerment, solidarity and renewal.
index.pillars_title: Five ways to hold the line.
index.sectors_lede: Every organisation in the ESRF.net community is classified across ten vital sectors — the domains where innovation and cooperation strengthen what matters most. Counts below represent the current atlas.
index.atlas_lede: Every pin is an organisation; every cluster, a city; every colour, a sector. The atlas is a living record of who sustains the daily machinery of a resilient Europe.
index.join_title: Does your organisation contribute to a Europe that holds?
index.join_sub: Claim a listing and take your place on the atlas. Joining the ESRF.net community is free for organisations whose work strengthens European security and resilience.
index.hero_deck_html: Where governments set policy and international bodies coordinate, ESRF.net unites the businesses, institutions and citizens whose daily work quietly sustains our shared life — a decentralised framework powered by the organisations that drive the economy.
index.hero_fig_caption: A community of practitioners, spanning thirty nations, bound by five moral principles.
index.foundation_body_1_html: ESRF.net is a decentralised network built for, and by, the entrepreneurs with local impact. Funded by Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund, we believe that security is not just a mandate for governments — it is the shared work of practitioners, innovators and communities who strengthen Europe through cooperation every day.
index.foundation_pull_html: Security is not just a mandate. It is a responsibility we carry, together.
index.foundation_body_3_html: Organisations are classified across ten vital sectors aligned with the EU NIS2 Directive and guided by five moral principles: vigilance, stewardship, empowerment, solidarity and renewal.
index.analytics_lede: Three figures, published each issue — where the community is concentrated, where new connections are forming, and how the ten sectors distribute across countries.
index.fig01_desc: A horizontal bar comparing the ten vital sectors. Emergency & Crisis Response leads; Energy & Grid Resilience is the newest frontier of growth.
index.fig02_desc: A bar across thirty nations. The Netherlands, United Kingdom and Germany carry the current weight of the network.
index.dispatch_lede: A curated feed of European security, resilience and crisis signals — filed under the sector each story touches. The full dispatch is updated continuously.
index.fig03_desc: How the ten sectors distribute across the fifteen most represented nations — specialisation vs. diversification at a glance.

directory.join_sub: Any organisation whose work contributes to European security and resilience is welcome in the ESRF.net community.

analytics.title: Analytics — ESRF.net
analytics.description: Data analytics for the ESRF.net community — sector distribution, country breakdown and community growth.
analytics.hero_deck: Three figures published with every issue. Where the community is concentrated, where new connections are forming, and how the ten sectors distribute across countries.
analytics.fig01_desc: Emergency responders and continuity professionals form the established core of the community; energy and transport represent the newest frontier of growth.
analytics.fig01_caption: Fig. 01 — Counts of organisations in each of the ten vital sectors. Bar colour follows the sector itself.
analytics.fig02_desc: Thirty nations, each contributing a share of the whole. The Netherlands, United Kingdom and Germany currently carry the most; Malta and Bulgaria the fewest — a balance the framework is actively redistributing.
analytics.fig02_caption: Fig. 02 — Distribution of listed organisations across the 30 countries represented on the atlas.
analytics.cta_sub: The dispatch files each day's European resilience signals to the sector it touches.
analytics.fig03_desc: How the ten vital sectors distribute across the fifteen most represented nations. Each bar is a country; each colour, a sector. Where one colour dominates, the local ecosystem is specialised; where the spread is even, it is diversified.
analytics.fig03_caption: Fig. 03 — Sector composition of the top fifteen countries by number of listed organisations. Bar colour follows the sector palette.

news.submit_sub: Organisations listed on the atlas can contribute stories, reports and updates to the ESRF.net dispatch.

about.title: Foundation — ESRF.net
about.description: About ESRF.net — its charter, five moral pillars and how organisations join the community for European security and resilience.
about.hero_deck: ESRF.net is built on a simple conviction: resilience grows where practitioners, innovators and communities work together. It is the daily work of organisations, institutions and citizens who strengthen Europe through cooperation, shared knowledge and collective ingenuity.
about.charter_quote: Security is not just a mandate. It is a responsibility we carry, together.
about.charter_line1: Security is not just a mandate.
about.what_sub: Decentralised by design, civic in purpose, European in reach.
about.what_body_1_html: ESRF.net is a curated directory of 1,931 organisations across 30 countries whose work, visible or otherwise, strengthens the continent’s capacity to innovate, cooperate and build lasting resilience through collective effort.
about.what_body_2_html: Where governments set policy and international bodies coordinate, ESRF.net unites the entrepreneurs, institutions and first responders whose daily practice is the actual substrate of resilience. We classify organisations across ten vital sectors — aligned with the EU NIS2 Directive.
about.what_pull_html: We are not a regulator. We are a community of practice.
about.what_body_3: Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund receives donations and contributions to strengthen the network and expand its reach. Listings are free; contributions are welcome; participation is a matter of stewardship, not subscription.
about.pillar1_body_html: To see clearly before others react. The first pillar is the patient, structural art of foresight — reading signals, mapping scenarios and building understanding that stays one step ahead. Vigilance is not anxiety; it is the quiet confidence of a community that pays attention together.
about.pillar2_body_html: To strengthen what sustains us. The second pillar is the discipline of reinforcing physical, digital and institutional foundations. Stewardship means one does not own what one protects; one holds it in trust for everyone who depends on it — and makes it stronger for those who come next.
about.pillar3_body_html: To stand ready, together. The third pillar is the cultivation of optionality — the capacity to act with confidence when a plan meets weather. Preparedness is not stockpiling; it is the shared competence to adapt.
about.pillar4_body_html: To act as one when it matters. The fourth pillar is the virtue of arriving — reliably, quickly, together. Solidarity is not a sentiment; it is a practice that must be rehearsed long before it is required.
about.pillar5_body_html: To rise stronger than before. The fifth pillar is the discipline of absorbing shocks and restarting systems with innovation at the core. Renewal treats every recovery as an opportunity to improve what the previous version could not — turning experience into engineering, and setbacks into breakthroughs.
about.join_sub: A listing is free. Stewardship is the only dues we collect.
about.join_body_1: Any organisation — commercial, institutional, non-profit — whose work strengthens European security and resilience is welcome on the atlas. There are three steps:
about.join_step_1_html: Request a listing. Fill in the request-a-listing form with your organisation’s name, a one-paragraph description and your primary sector of practice.
about.join_step_2_html: Align with the charter. Every listed organisation subscribes to the ESRF.net charter — one page, no fine print.
about.join_step_3_html: Appear on the atlas. Your listing goes live on the directory, the map and, where relevant, the dispatch.
about.join_body_2: Contributions to the foundation — financial or in-kind — are welcome but never required. ESRF.net does not sell listings, rank its members, or charge subscription fees.
about.governance_sub: Governance, methodology, selection — all in the open.
about.gov_entity_body: ESRF.net is operated by Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund, a not-for-profit foundation registered in the Netherlands (KvK). The foundation — referred to as ESRF.net when acting as community enabler — exists solely to maintain the framework, fund the editorial operation, and protect the independence of the directory.
about.gov_methodology_body_html: Organisations are classified across ten vital sectors aligned with the EU NIS2 Directive and the Critical Entities Resilience (CER) Directive. Each listing is editorially reviewed against three criteria: (1) the organisation's work must demonstrably contribute to European security or resilience, (2) it must operate in or serve at least one of the 30 covered countries, and (3) it must have a verifiable public presence (website, chamber of commerce registration, or equivalent).
about.gov_selection_body: ESRF.net does not sell listings, rank its members, or charge subscription fees. Organisations may request a listing or be identified through open-source intelligence. Every listing is reviewed by the editorial team against the sector taxonomy and the three criteria above. Listings that no longer meet the criteria are archived, not deleted — transparency applies in both directions.
about.gov_stichting_body: The foundation (Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund) is the legal entity. The network is the community of 1,931 organisations. The foundation does not own, control or represent the organisations it lists — it curates the directory, maintains the atlas, publishes the dispatch, and channels contributions into initiatives that make the network grow and more robust. The organisations themselves are sovereign; the directory is a public good.
about.gov_contact_body_html:
Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund · The Netherlands
General enquiries: hello@esrf.net
Privacy matters: privacy@esrf.net
about.prevent_body: To see clearly before others react — foresight as a collective strength, not a reaction to fear.
about.protect_body: To strengthen what sustains us — reinforcing critical functions, vital processes and shared foundations.

footer.brand_desc: ESRF.net — European security and resilience, powered by 1,931 organisations across 30 countries.

pillar.prevent_body: To see clearly before others react — foresight as a collective strength, not a reaction to fear.
pillar.protect_body: To strengthen what sustains us — reinforcing critical functions, vital processes and shared foundations.
pillar.prepare_body: To stand ready, together — through training, innovation and sustainable preparedness.
pillar.respond_body: To act as one when it matters — reliable networks, rapid coordination, mutual support.
pillar.recover_body: To rise stronger than before — absorb shocks, innovate through recovery, and renew the system.

form.hero_deck: Any organisation whose work strengthens European security and resilience is welcome in the ESRF.net community. Submissions are reviewed by the editorial team within three working days. Listing is free — we ask only that your work genuinely contributes to one of our ten vital sectors.
form.label_gdpr: I agree to the privacy policy and consent to the storage of this data for listing review purposes.
form.placeholder_description: One or two sentences describing what your organisation does…
form.hint_website: Must begin with https:// or http://
form.success_body: Thank you for your submission. The ESRF.net editorial team will review your listing within three working days. You will receive a confirmation at the email address provided.
form.err_too_fast: Please take a moment to review the form before submitting.
form.err_required: Please fill in all required fields.
form.err_gdpr: Please accept the privacy policy to continue.
form.err_url: Please enter a valid website URL starting with https:// or http://
form.err_email: Please enter a valid email address.
form.err_generic: An error occurred. Please try again.
form.err_network: Network error — please check your connection and try again.

modal.title: Claim your listing
modal.intro: Any organisation whose work strengthens European security and resilience is welcome. Listing is free. Submissions are reviewed within three working days.

sponsor.hero_deck: Back the network that connects Europe's security and resilience community — 1,931 organisations, 30 countries, ten vital sectors. Your support keeps the atlas independent, open and growing.
sponsor.why_title: Built on independence, sustained by patronage.
sponsor.why_body_1: ESRF.net is editorially independent. We accept no government mandates, no advertising, no pay-to-rank listings. The only way that works is through transparent patronage from organisations who believe in the mission.
sponsor.why_body_2: ESRF.net reaches security and resilience professionals across 30 European nations — procurement managers, policy advisers, crisis coordinators, and business leaders. A sponsorship aligns your brand with Europe's most purposeful network in this field.
sponsor.why_body_3: We publish transparently: every sponsor is named, every tier disclosed. ESRF.net's editorial positions are never influenced by commercial relationships.
sponsor.tiers_title: Three ways to be part of it.
sponsor.tier1_tagline: “The organisations that made the atlas possible.”
sponsor.tier1_benefit1: Logo on home page, directory and all country pages
sponsor.tier1_benefit2: Named in every issue of the ESRF.net Dispatch
sponsor.tier1_benefit3: Logo on printed and digital materials
sponsor.tier1_benefit4: Founding Patron badge on your listing
sponsor.tier1_benefit5: Dedicated thank-you editorial in year one
sponsor.tier1_benefit6: Invitation to shape the editorial roadmap
sponsor.tier2_tagline: "Aligned with a vital sector of your choice."
sponsor.tier2_benefit1: Logo on the sector pages you select
sponsor.tier2_benefit2: Named in Dispatch issues covering your sector
sponsor.tier2_benefit3: Programme Partner badge on your listing
sponsor.tier3_benefit1: Named in the ESRF.net supporters section
sponsor.tier3_benefit2: Community Supporter badge on your listing
sponsor.no_sponsors: Our founding patrons are currently being selected. Enquire below to be among the first.
sponsor.success_body: Thank you for your interest in supporting ESRF.net. A member of the team will be in touch within three working days.
sponsor.faq1_a: No. ESRF.net is editorially independent. Sponsors are acknowledged transparently, but have no influence over the directory or Dispatch editorial.
sponsor.faq2_q: How long does a sponsorship last?
sponsor.faq2_a: Sponsorship agreements are annual. Renewals are offered to current sponsors first, before new openings are announced publicly.
sponsor.faq3_q: Can we sponsor a specific country or sector?
sponsor.faq3_a: Yes — Programme Partner sponsorships can be aligned to a specific vital sector of your choice. If your organisation operates in, say, Critical Infrastructure or Health & Medical Manufacturing, your logo will appear on those section pages. Discuss this in your enquiry and we will tailor the arrangement accordingly.
sponsor.faq4_q: What payment methods do you accept?
sponsor.faq4_a: We accept bank transfer (SEPA and international wire) and invoice billing. Full details are provided after agreement is signed.
sponsor.faq5_q: Is ESRF.net open to organisations outside Europe?
sponsor.faq5_a: No. Like donations, sponsorships are exclusively available to European organisations and existing ESRF.net network members. This ensures our funding remains fully aligned with our European mission.
sponsor.placeholder_message: Tell us about your organisation and why you'd like to support ESRF.net…

consent.text: ESRF.net uses minimal advertising to support its mission. We use cookies for personalised ads. You can accept or decline.
ads.label: Advertisement
sponsor.why_body_1: ESRF.net is editorially independent. We accept no government mandates and no pay-to-rank listings. The directory is curated by a small team of practitioners who know this space — and who intend to keep it that way. While we display minimal, non-intrusive advertising to sustain operations, the true engine behind this network is transparent patronage from organisations who believe in the mission.

fund.hero_deck: ESRF.net is sustained by the European community it serves. Not by a handful of large donors, but by the collective power of many small contributions. Donations are accepted exclusively from organisations and individuals within Europe and from ESRF.net network members. This is a deliberate choice — independence starts with how you are funded.
fund.idea_sub: Many small contributions, one resilient network.
fund.idea_body_1_html: Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund is a not-for-profit foundation. It does not sell listings, charge membership fees, or accept pay-to-rank arrangements. The directory is — and will remain — a public good.
fund.idea_body_2: To cover its operating costs, ESRF.net is funded through three channels: minimal, non-intrusive advertising (via Google AdSense), voluntary donations from the European community, and corporate sponsorships. Advertising revenue helps keep the lights on; donations and sponsorships fund the growth and editorial independence of the network.
fund.idea_body_2b: The foundation is designed around a principle: many small contributions are more powerful than a few large ones. When hundreds of organisations and individuals each give a modest amount, the network becomes self-sustaining, independent, and accountable to the community — not to any single benefactor. This distributed funding model mirrors the distributed resilience the framework itself promotes. It is not just a funding strategy; it is the philosophy made practical.
fund.idea_pull_html: One donation is a gesture. A thousand donations are an unstoppable force.
fund.idea_eligibility_html: European contributions only. To preserve the independence and European character of the framework, the foundation accepts donations exclusively from organisations and individuals based in Europe, and from members of the ESRF.net network.
fund.idea_body_3: Every euro contributed goes to maintaining the atlas, curating the directory, publishing the Dispatch, and expanding the reach of the network across 30 European countries. There are no hidden overheads — transparency is the only policy.
fund.where_intro: Every contribution to the foundation is allocated across four pillars of operation. No funds go to shareholder returns — there are none.
fund.alloc1_body: Editorial curation, verification of listings, data quality, and the interactive map infrastructure.
fund.alloc2_body: Servers, multilingual infrastructure across 27 languages, security, and continuous platform development.
fund.alloc3_body: Publishing the security and resilience bulletin, country analytics, and network communications.
fund.alloc4_body: Foundation administration, compliance, financial reporting, and the annual transparency report.
fund.ways_sub: Financial, practical, or simply spreading the word.
fund.way1_body: A single contribution of any amount. Whether it is €5 or €500 — every donation is recorded transparently and channelled directly into the network's operations. No amount is too small.
fund.way2_body: A modest recurring contribution — even €2 per month — gives the foundation something more valuable than any large sum: predictability. Predictable income means better planning: more languages, more listings verified, more countries covered. A community that contributes steadily builds more than one that depends on windfalls.
fund.way3_body_html: Organisations seeking a deeper partnership can explore our sponsorship tiers — Founding Patron, Programme Partner, or Community Supporter. Sponsorships are disclosed transparently and never influence editorial decisions.
fund.way4_body: Not every contribution is monetary. Translation help, data verification, editorial input, or technical expertise — the community's skills are the network's greatest asset. Contact us if you would like to contribute your time or expertise.
fund.way5_body: Share ESRF.net with colleagues, partners, and networks. Every new listing, every new reader, every shared article strengthens the community. Visibility is a form of contribution.
fund.impact1: Covers the hosting cost of one country page for a month.
fund.impact2: Funds the editorial review and verification of five new listings.
fund.impact3: Sustains the Dispatch bulletin for one edition — research, writing, and distribution.
fund.impact4: Enables the translation of the entire platform into one additional European language.
fund.transparency_sub: Every euro accounted for, every decision disclosed.
fund.transparency_body_1: Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund publishes an annual transparency report detailing all income, expenditures, and allocation decisions. The foundation is registered in the Netherlands and subject to Dutch non-profit governance law.
fund.transparency_body_2: No individual donor — however generous — is granted editorial influence. The separation between funding and editorial is absolute: contributors fund the infrastructure, the editorial team guards the independence. This is not a policy; it is the reason the network exists.
fund.transparency_item1: Annual financial report published on this website
fund.transparency_item2: All sponsorships and major donations disclosed by name and tier
fund.transparency_item3: Foundation registered as Stichting under Dutch law (KvK)
fund.transparency_item4: No shareholder returns — 100 % of income goes to network operations
fund.transparency_item5: Minimal, non-intrusive advertising (Google AdSense) to sustain baseline operations
fund.transparency_item6: Donations accepted exclusively from European sources and ESRF.net network members
fund.cta_body_html: Donations are handled through the foundation's bank account via SEPA transfer. For recurring gifts or questions about contributing, please contact us directly.
fund.cta_eligibility_html: Eligibility: The foundation accepts donations exclusively from organisations and individuals based in Europe, and from members of the ESRF.net network. Contributions from outside this scope will be respectfully declined.
fund.cta_contact_html: Questions or prefer another way to contribute? Contact us at hello@esrf.net.
fund.faq1_a: ESRF.net accepts donations exclusively from organisations and individuals based in Europe, and from members of the ESRF.net network regardless of location. This policy exists to preserve the European character and independence of the framework. Contributions from outside this scope will be respectfully declined.
fund.faq2_a: Stichting ESRF European Security and Resilience Fund is a Dutch not-for-profit foundation. Tax deductibility depends on your country of residence and local tax law. We recommend consulting your tax adviser. The foundation can provide a donation receipt upon request.
fund.faq3_a: Yes. Anonymous donations are accepted and respected. For the annual transparency report, the foundation will report the total amount of anonymous contributions without naming individual donors. If you prefer your name to be listed, please let us know.
fund.faq4_q: Does my donation influence the directory?
fund.faq4_a: Absolutely not. The separation between funding and editorial is unconditional. Donations — of any size — do not influence which organisations are listed, how they are described, or what the Dispatch covers. This independence is non-negotiable.
fund.faq5_q: What is the difference between a donation and a sponsorship?
fund.faq5_a_html: A donation is a contribution without conditions — any amount, no visibility expectations. A sponsorship is a structured partnership with defined tiers, brand visibility, and an annual agreement. Both are disclosed in the transparency report; neither grants editorial influence.
fund.faq6_q: How often is the transparency report published?
fund.faq6_a: Annually, after the close of the financial year. The report is published on this website and includes total income, expenditure breakdown, a list of disclosed sponsors, and a narrative account of how funds were used to grow the network.

editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html: Since the end of February 2026, the Strait of Hormuz has been blocked as a result of the armed conflict between the United States, Israel and Iran.[1] [2] This blockade affects the main transit route for oil from the Middle East and thus impacts the entire European energy supply.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_2: Shell CEO Wael Sawan warned on 24 March that Europe could expect physical shortages from April onwards — first with kerosene, then diesel and finally petrol.[3] [4] In March, global oil production fell by around 10.1 million barrels a day, one of the most severe supply shocks in decades. [5]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_3: On 11 March, the International Energy Agency (IEA) released a record 400 million barrels from strategic reserves.[7][6] In the Netherlands, phase 1 of the National Oil Crisis Plan (LCP-O) came into effect on 17 April: there are no acute shortages as yet, but the government is stepping up monitoring and consulting with major consumers in the transport sector.[8] [9] The European Commission does not currently anticipate an immediate shortage, but is preparing a package of measures.[10] [11]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.h2_2: What does this mean for Europe — and for businesses?
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_4: At European level, efforts are being focused on three areas: supply measures such as the release of strategic reserves and additional LNG imports; demand reduction through reduced travel and working from home; and phased contingency plans for each Member State.[9][12][6] Energy Commissioner Dan Jørgensen is preparing the EU for what he calls a “prolonged” energy crisis.[13] The Dutch LCP-O has four escalation phases, with the government focusing primarily on voluntary measures and communication up to and including the most severe phase. [8]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_5: For businesses, it boils down to this: reducing fuel consumption, combining journeys, taking a critical look at the vehicle fleet, spreading out deliveries, and exploring alternative routes and energy sources. But the most important thing is to communicate clearly and in good time with customers about what is changing and why.[14] [15]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.tip_title: Communicate early on — don’t wait until there’s a problem
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_6: In a post-pandemic market, customers interpret silence as bad news.[14] Keep them proactively informed as soon as you spot risks in your supply chain, even if you cannot yet provide a precise schedule.[15] Never[16] wait until a delivery fails.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_7: Don’t focus on your own internal issues. Explain exactly what the oil shortage means for their order, delivery time or price.[14]Keep it brief and outline the options available to the customer.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.tip_title_3: Be transparent about the causes and the uncertainties
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_8: Explain that the situation stems from a geopolitical shock — the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz — and that governments are also facing uncertainty.[1] [10] Customers are more likely to accept bad news if they understand the background and feel that you are being completely open with them.[16] [17]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_9: Use specific facts and figures: "delivery times will increase from 3 to 7 working days" or "fuel surcharge of X% from 1 May".[17]Phrases such as "possible slight delay" actually cause more anxiety than they provide clarity.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.tip_title_5: Back up price rises with facts
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_10: Many businesses are having to raise their prices due to increased fuel and input costs. Be honest about this rather than glossing over it.[16] Cite specific figures — such as kerosene prices having doubled or the diesel price index — so that customers can see that this is not an arbitrary price hike. [3]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_11: Give customers options: alternative products, bundled deliveries, flexible delivery dates, digital alternatives to in-person visits, or longer-term contracts that offer price certainty.[14] When people have[17] a choice, they feel less like victims.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_12: Identify where your target audience gets their information — newsletters, WhatsApp, LinkedIn, customer portals — and communicate consistently through those channels.[15]KFC handled its 2018 chicken crisis well by responding actively on social media and maintaining a live update of which branches were open. [18]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_13: Show that you understand the customer’s frustration. A phrase such as “I understand how important this delivery is to you” goes further than a discount.[17]Empathy builds trust in a way that promotions cannot.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.tip_title_9: Link your message to sustainability — but only if it makes sense
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_14: Operational changes such as reducing the number of journeys, driving more economically and electrifying the vehicle fleet often align with existing sustainability targets.[12] [13] Communicate this, but only if it is transparent and credible. Greenwashing is severely penalised.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_15: Develop contingency plans now for scenarios such as price rises, delivery delays and distribution disruptions. Use flexible templates, not rigid scripts.[16] [17] Train your customer service team to maintain the right tone, show empathy and provide up-to-date information, so that they do not give out conflicting information.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_16: Subject: Update on deliveries — fuel supply situation in Europe
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_2: As you are probably aware, the European fuel supply is under pressure due to the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. We would like to keep you informed in good time about what this means for your deliveries.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.li_html: From [date], delivery times may increase from 3 to 5 or 7 working days.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.li_html_2: Fuel surcharge — from [date], we will apply a surcharge of [X%] to transport-sensitive orders, based on the current diesel price index. This surcharge is reviewed on a monthly basis.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.li_html_3: Consolidated deliveries — to minimise the number of journeys, we group orders by region. You can specify a preferred delivery date.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_3: We are following the National Oil Crisis Plan and optimising our logistics: route planning, fleet management and alternative suppliers. Our aim is to minimise the impact on you as much as possible.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_4: You will receive an update every two weeks via [email / customer portal]. If you have any questions about an order in the meantime, please feel free to contact us on [phone number] or via [email address].
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_5: We appreciate your understanding and trust.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_20: This message combines the key elements of effective crisis communication: providing information at an early stage, being transparent, keeping things concrete, prioritising the customer’s perspective, and offering a way forward.[15][14] [17]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.li_html_4: Reduce and optimise your vehicle fleet — consider a mobility budget instead of company cars. [12]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.li_html_5: Fill up outside motorways, combine journeys by region and reward fuel-efficient driving. [12]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.li: Carry out technical adjustments: remove roof panels, check tyre pressure, use a lower-viscosity engine oil.
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.li_html_6: Join the large-scale consumers’ consultation group and the trade associations that are involved in discussions with the government regarding the LCP-O. [8]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.li_html_7: Monitor decisions by the EU Coordination Group and IEA market reports for early indicators.[10] [5]
editorial_oil_shortage_2026.p_html_21: The better prepared you are internally, the more credible your external communications will be.
